coredata.dc:title: Origin of high storage capacity in N-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Miah, Milon
Bhattacharya, Shatabda
Gupta, Abhisek
Saha, Shyamal K.
coredata.dc:description: Study of high performance supercapacitor using doping and edge states in graphene is an interesting area of research, however, the storage capacity and understanding of the origin of enhancement are still in its infant stage. In the present work, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (N_GQD) is synthesized to enhance the storage capacity exploiting both the doping level as well as edge states. The results are compared with undoped graphene quantum dot and doped large graphene sheet. It is seen that the highest value of average specific capacitance (509Fg−1) is achieved in case of N_GQD. The origin of this high performance is explained on the basis of trap states created by both dopants and edge states which can adsorb charge carriers to enhance the storage capacity .The charge transport in N_GQD sample is also studied on the basis of trap induced electron transport.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Trap state
Charge transport
Supercapacitor
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Temperature and voltage dependent charge trapping-detrapping mechanism
Modification of charge transport due to presence of trap states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) N_GQDs and (b) U_GQDs with inset shows size distribution histogram.
(a), (b) FTIR spectra of N_GQD and U_GQD. (c) Raman spectra of N_GQD and U_GQD.

(a) Full range XPS spectra for N_GQD; (b) High resolution Nitrogen spectrum and deconvolution of it. (c) Full range spectra for U_GQD; (d) High resolution of carbon spectrum and deconvolution of it. Proposed N_GQD structure based on the result of XPS analysis. (e) Pyridinic like structures at the basal planes, (f) Pyrrolic like structure forming at the edges.

J–V characteristics at different temperatures for (a) N_GQD and (b) U_GQD; high bias voltage
ln J
versus
ln V
plot for (c) N_GQD sample; (d) conductivity versus temperature curve for N_GQD sample.
Device fabrication of the two electrode configuration.
Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves for (a,b) N_GQD; (c,d) N_GS; and (e,f) U_GQD respectively.
Cyclic voltammograms (CV) for (a) N_GQD; (b) N_GS; (c) U_GQD. (d) Nyquist impedance plot of different electrodes.

head.ce:title: Origin of high storage capacity in N-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Jadavpur, Kolkata 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Study of high performance supercapacitor using doping and edge states in graphene is an interesting area of research, however, the storage capacity and understanding of the origin of enhancement are still in its infant stage. In the present work, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (N_GQD) is synthesized to enhance the storage capacity exploiting both the doping level as well as edge states. The results are compared with undoped graphene quantum dot and doped large graphene sheet. It is seen that the highest value of average specific capacitance (509Fg−1) is achieved in case of N_GQD. The origin of this high performance is explained on the basis of trap states created by both dopants and edge states which can adsorb charge carriers to enhance the storage capacity .The charge transport in N_GQD sample is also studied on the basis of trap induced electron transport.

ce:section.ce:para: Besides multifunctional applications viz. electronics [1–3] magnetic [4–6] optoelectronics [7–10] and sensing [11–13] applications, a major role of graphene exploiting large surface area lies with the supercapacitive application. Supercapacitors are also known as ultracapacitors that can store greater amount of energy than conventional capacitors and they can often be charged and discharged in seconds with extremely long cycle life. Many attempts have been made to enhance the storage capacity and particularly, since the discovery of graphene, research on graphene based supercapacitors exploiting large surface area plays an important role [14–21]. Further enhancement in storage capacity was realized introducing new states by means of dopants or creating large number of edge states which can store charge carriers in graphene based supercapacitor [22,23]. The remarkable doping effect in carbon nanotubes has stimulated to exploit the same mechanism in graphene to enhance the storage capacity [24–27]. Chemical doping of nitrogen is found to be an effective tool to intrinsically modify graphene since nitrogen chemically binds to the carbon lattice of graphene. Four different types of dopants viz. pyridine-like (N-6), pyrrole-like (N-5), quaternary (N-Q) and oxidized nitrogen (N-O) are obtained depending upon the synthesis technique [28]. All these doped states serve as additional trap centers which can store charge carriers to enhance the storage capacity.

Another strategy to enhance the storage capacity is the creation of edge states in graphene. To increase the number of edge states, size reduction in graphene sheet is an elegant approach. Size reduction of graphene sheet or realization of graphene quantum dots (GQD) offer larger proportion of edge states which also serve as trap states to store charge carriers for supercapacitive application [22,29]. Therefore, exploiting both the effects of doping as well as edge states, in the present work, we have synthesized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (N_GQD) to enhance the storage capacity by (509Fg−1) in the as-synthesized N_GQD. The results are also compared with undoped graphene quantum dot and doped graphene sheet. Considering the doped and edge states as trap centers, the charge transport in these materials is also investigated on the basis of trap-induced electron transport.

Ultrafine graphite powder (Merck), potassium permanganate (Merck), concentrated sulfuric acid (Merck, 98% pure), hydrogen peroxide (30%, Merck), hydrochloric acid (35%, Merck), Hydrazine hydrate (Merck), N,N-Dimethyl formamide (Merck), Ammonia solution (Merck) and double distilled water are used to synthesize the materials.
Graphene oxide (GO) is prepared as reported elsewhere from natural purified graphite powder by modified Hummer’s method [30].

Undopped Graphene quantum dots were prepared following the same procedure as metioned in our previus paper [31]. A GO solution (5ml) was mixed with DMF (50ml) in a beaker and subjected to ultrasonication for 30minutes in a cold water bath to yield a clear homogeneous yellow brown dispersion. After that 10ml of hydrazine hydrate was added to it and further sonicated for 180minutes. The black product floated on the solution was washed thoroughly with distilled water and alcohol and dried under vacuum at 370K for 2hours.

In a typical procedure for Nitrogen doped Graphene sheets, the as-synthesized GO was diluted with deionized water to 1.0mgmL−1 and sonicated for 10min to create a homogeneous graphene oxide dispersion. Then, 5ml of ammonia was added into the GO dispersion and stirred at 80°C for 12h. The final product was collected and washed several times using centrifuge at 12,000rpm and dried at 60°C [32].

5ml of GO solution was mixed with DMF (50ml) in a beaker and stirred for 30min to obtain a clear homogeneous yellow brown dispersion. After that ammonia was added dropwise to the solution untill the pH of the solution becomes 10. Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon coated stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 180°C in a vacuum oven and kept for 12hours. The final product was collected and washed several times using centrifuge at 12000rpm. The synthesis procedure is shown in Fig. 1 .

To characterize the size of graphene quantum dots, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was carried out using JEOL-2011 high resolution transmission electron microscope. FTIR measurements were carried out by a NICOLET MAGNA IR 750 system. Raman spectra were measured by a micro-Raman spectrometer (model JYT-6400). The sample for Raman measurements was excited at 514nm. The X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated using OMICRON-0571 system. Electrical measurements for electrode materials in the form of pellet have been carried out using Keithley 617 programmable electrometer for dc measurements and Agilent E4980A precision LCR Meter for ac measurements. Electrochemical performance of the supercapacitor cells was tested by cyclic voltammetry (Versastat 3, Princeton applied research) and galvanostic charge/discharge was investigated by NEWARE BTS battery tester. Electrochemical performance was also carried out by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on an CHI 660E electrochemical workstation.

The morphology of as prepared N_GQDs and U_GQDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From Fig. 2 (a, b) it can be seen that the N_GQDs and U_GQDs have a uniform size distribution between 2–5nm without any apparent aggregation.

The functional group present in U_GQD and N_GQD are further confirmed by the FTIR analysis. Fig. 3 (a, b) show the FTIR spectra for N_GQD and U_GQD. The FTIR analysis of U_GQD reveals that there are two main functional groups, viz. COOH and C-OH present at the edge and on the surface respectively of the quantum dots in which the peak at 3430cm−1 is due to the presence of the OH group. The Peak for graphitic CC appears at 1628cm−1 and symmetric stretching vibration peak of COO bond appears at 1400cm−1. Characteristic deformation of C-OH group appears at 1272cm−1 [33]. For N_GQD, the broad peak in the region 3600–1800cm−1 is due to the presence of the OH, and NH2 groups. The peaks at ∼1624cm−1 and 1420cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of graphitic CC and symmetric stretching vibration peaks of COO bond. The peaks at 1100–1260cm−1 are due to OH deformation and C-N stretching vibrations [34].

Raman spectroscopy is used to further examine the edge states and doping effect of U_GQD and N_GQD as shown in Fig. 3(c) After N doping i.e. in case of N_GQD, the position of the G-band is significantly blue-shifted. The D and G peaks of U_GQD appear at 1354 and 1581cm−1 respectively, whereas the D and G peaks for nitrogen doped N_GQD appear at 1365 and 1605cm−1 respectively. This type of blue shift in the G-band position has been attributed to the phonon stiffening with nitrogen doping [35]. The intensity ratio of ID/IG is changed from1.066 (U_GQD) to 1.157 after doping by nitrogen. Basically, this change in ID/IG ratio can be ascribed to the physical and structural changes in the graphene skeleton. This observation implies that the doping of N atoms results in the increase of the defects in comparison with U_GQD [36].

Furthermore, to determine the chemical composition of N_GQDs, we have measured the XPS spectra of this sample as shown in Fig. 4 (a, b). The N_GQDs produced from these methods exhibit four different types of nitrogen bonding configurations: pyridine-like (N-6), pyrrole-like (N-5), quaternary (N-Q) and oxidized nitrogen (N-O). Fig. 4(a) shows the XPS result of the N_GQD and it clearly shows the appearance of a peak at about 400eV which corresponds to nitrogen. In addition, there is also some residual oxygen and carbon peaks in the sample. Fig. 4(b) shows the high resolution spectra spectra of the nitrogen peak for the sample N_GQD. The results of deconvolution of these spectra show that nitrogen is bonded to the graphite network in four different forms including pyridinic, pyrrolic, quaternary and oxidized nitrogen, for which the peaks appear at 398.2eV, 401eV, 402.3eV and 404.02eV respectively [28]. From the intensity distribution of the main nitrogen peak it is seen that for pyrrolic (N-5) structure the highest intensity is obtained. It means that the probability to form the pyrrolic structure is suitable at edges rather than at basal plane. At basal plane pyridinic (N-6) structure is formed.

In order to compare the elemental analysis, we have also performed XPS of U_GQD sample as shown in Fig. 4(c, d). From this it is seen that the main intense peaks are due to carbon and oxygen groups mainly. There is no signature of nitrogen element presents in the sample. Hence it is due to pure (undoped) graphene state. The high resolution peak of carbon is deconvoluted from which it is seen that CC (284.5eV), CO (285.7eV), CC (288.9eV), bonds are present due to graphitic network [37].

Therefore, from XPS analysis of N_GQD it is noticed that at the edges nitrogen doping is much more effective rather than basal plane. In N_GQD, the ratio of edge atom to basal plane atom is much more higher, indicating the doping effect more pronounced at the edge as evident from the intensity distribution curves of XPS. The formation of pyrrolic and pyridinic like structutres in the present N_GQD sample is shown schematically in Fig. 4(e, f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature and voltage dependent charge trapping-detrapping mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: As reported earlier, several defect states in graphene lattice have been treated as trap states to explain the dielectric [38] and supercapacitive [29,39] behavior in highly defective reduced graphene oxide. In order to understand the insight of trap states, in the present case, we have measured temperature dependent current-voltage (I–V) characteristics for both the samples [39]. Fig. 5 (a,b) show the current density as a function of voltage from which it is seen that the nature of the curves are nonlinear and can be expressed by a simple power law J ∞ Vm, where m denotes the nonlinearity of the curve. From the Figures it is also seen that at low bias voltage, curves are almost linear which signifies that the conduction process is mainly ohmic leading to a value of m equal to 1. While at higher bias voltage, conduction occurs through trap states and nonlinearity appears, leading to m value away from 1. It is also seen that, the curves are symmetric on both sides which are explained on the basis of space charge limited conduction mechanism (SCLC). In addition to this, it is seen that as temperature decreases, nonlinearity starts from low bias voltage i.e. at lower temperature, conduction is mediated predominantly via trap states. At room temperature, thermal energy of the electrons is high enough to be the trap states even the deep trap levels empty. As temperature is reduced, thermal agitation of the electron is decreased and trap states start to fill up. As seen from the theoretically fitted curves given in Fig. 5(a) the value of m for N_GQD obtained as 4.46 at lowest temperature 50K and that for U_GQD in Fig. 5(b) it is found to be 2.54 at 50K. Therefore, primarily from the value of m as obtained here we can see that in case of N_GQD the conduction process is mostly trap states mediated.

Now in order to quantitative evaluation of trap states, in the present GQD sample we have used J-V equation as follows [40]:
(1)
J =
{
( μ
N
v
/
q
l − 1
)
(
(
2 l + 1
)
/
(
l + 1
) )
1 / l
( ( ε
ε
0
/
N t
)
(
l /
(
l + 1
)
) )
l
(
V
l + 1
/
d
2 l + 1
)
}

where, μ is the mobility of free electron,
N v
is the effective density of states, l is an exponent, q is the electronic charge,
N t
is the trap density, and ε is the dielectric constant. For N_GQD sample, we have fitted the data with the above equation and plot it in ln-ln scale as shown in Fig. 5(c). The value of ‘ l ’ is related to ‘m’ as m=
l
+1. In the high temperature limit, when most of the trap states are empty m is in the vicinity of 2, while at low temperatures, exponentially distributed trap states start to fill with charge carriers and m=(l +1)>2. As observed for U_GQD, in the J-V curve, the value of m is less than 2 indicating the fact that here the conduction is not mediated via exponentially distributed trap states. But in case of N_GQD, at room temperature the value of m is 2 and in order to evaluate the number density of trap states we have used the above equation given by equation 1. After fitting, the average trap density at room temperature is found to be 4.15×1012 m−3. The variation of trap density with temperature is given in Table 1 (is shown in the supporting information). At room temperature, most of the trap states are empty and as temperature is reduced, trap states start to fill up by charge carriers as evident from the J-V curves. The high density of trap states for this N_GQD sample is attributed to the defect sites that are present in this case. Also for N_GQD, the edge states are very much active as the number of edge atoms is larger compared to the surface atoms at the basal plane. In this the edge sites have the ability to accumulate more charges than the basal plane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of charge transport due to presence of trap states
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to understand the overall charge transport through the system, we have measured dc conductivity over the temperature range from 3K to 300K. From the temperature dependent conducting curve shown in Fig. 5(d) it is clear that the overall transport do not follow the conventional charge transport in disordered semiconductor solids viz. activating process, power law behavior in the critical regime of the Metal-insulator transition or Mott’s variable range hopping (VRH) as evident from Fig. S1 (is shown in the supporting information). It is usually known that in disorder systems, Mott’s variable range hopping is dominated in the low temperature region, however, power law behavior is followed by weakly localized system near M-I transition. To reveal the actual conductivity behavior over the entire temperature range, in the present case, we have considered a combination of low temperature region followed by VRH and a high temperature region followed by power law behavior. In addition to these conventional processes, we have added temperature dependent trapping-detrapping factors. In the low temperature range, most of the charge carriers (electrons) remain trapped at the trap centers and therefore conduction is mediated via these trap states at low temperature. While at higher temperature region most of the electrons are detrapped due to higher thermal energy and follows power law behavior in the critical regime of M-I transition. We have considered the trapping-detrapping factor as exponentially dependent on temperature and added this contribution to obtain the resultant behavior as [16]:
(2)
σ =
c
1
exp
( -
θ
1
T
)
T γ
+
c
2
exp
(
θ
1
T
)
( −
(
θ
2
T
)
1 / 3
)
Where
c 1
exp
(
-
θ 1
T
)
is the contribution of detrapped electrons at high temperature with a prefactor
c 1
and
c
2
exp
(
θ
1
T
)
gives that of trapped electrons at low temperature with a prefactor
c 2
.
θ 1
,
θ 2
are the constants depending on trap energy and hopping energy respectively. The experimental and theoretical curves agree well as shown in Fig. 5(d) the parameters are listed in Table 2 (is shown in the supporting information).

Electrode material characterization is performed using a two electrode configuration as shown in Fig. 6 the cell was fabricated using steel coin separated by a spacer. This cell was inserted between the two thick copper plates and tightened with screws. The two-electrode cell configuration was used for the measurements because it provided the most accurate result for performance of electrochemical capacitors. Ultra-fine powders of N_GQD, N_GS and U_GQD were mixed with acetylene black to increase the overall conductivity of the system. The performance of the supercapacitor was analyzed using Galvanostatic charge/discharge, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In order to evaluate specific capacitance of the electrodes we have used CV and charge/discharge curves. Fig. 7 shows the capacitive behavior as a function of time for the three samples. For N_GQD, N_GS and U_GQD the total time for 10 cycles are 4336, 2115, and 1563seconds respectively. Specific capacitance is calculated using the formula Csp
=
2 (iΔt/mΔv), where i is the current in mA, Δt is the discharging time in sec, m is the mass of active element in mg and Δv is the change in voltage in volt. This has been done using a current density of 1.6Ag−1. After calculation, the average specific capacitance for N_GS and U_GQD are found to be 256Fg−1 and 202Fg−1 respectively whereas for N_GQD this is evaluated as 509Fg−1. So far the literature is concerned, for GQD, the highest specific capacitance reported was 236Fg−1 [9]. Here we have obtained the value of 509Fg−1 which is almost double than that of the normal graphene quantum dots. Earlier, it was mentioned that for normal graphene quantum dots, the effect of edge states was responsible for this kind of enhancement. In our case, both the edge sites as well as doped states which act as trap states contributes to enhance this charge storage capacity.

Another method (CV method) was utilized for evaluation of specific capacitance of the samples. Fig. 8 shows the CV curves at different scan rates in 1M H2SO4 solution in the potential range from −2V to 2V for the two samples. Specific capacitance is also calculated from these CV curves using the formula;
C ( F
g
− 1
) =
1
m v (
V 2
−
V 1
)
∫
V 1
V 2
I ( V )
d v
; where ‘ m ’ is the mass of the active material, v is the scan rate (mV s−1),
V 1
and
V 2
are the integration limits and
∫
V 1
V 2
I ( V )
d v
is the integrated area under the CV curve [41]. The difference in the relative specific capacitances can be evaluated by comparing the area under the CV curve within the potential window of ±2V. The area under the CV curve for N_GQD is larger than that of N_GS and U_GQD as evident from Fig. 8 and the average value of specific capacitance for N_GQD, N_GS and U_GQD are found to be 458Fg−1, 270Fg−1, and 210Fg−1 respectively. Therefore, apart from charging discharging curves, CV also proves that N_GQD is electrochemically more active than N_GS and U_GQD. However, there are some small peaks observed in the CV curves which might be due to small pseudocapacitance effect [42,43] arising as a result of edge states and doping effects in graphene quantum dots and use of aqueous electrolyte[22,44].

We further used impedance spectroscopy to investigate the N_GQD and U_GQD electrode. Fig. 8(d) depicts the Nyquist plot of those electrodes. In the high frequency region, the Nyquist plot starts from the Z′-axis and progresses almost vertically to the Z″-axis at the low frequency, indicative of the ideal capacitive nature of the system. The series resistances of the N_GQD, N_GS and U_GQD are determined from the intercept of the Nyquist plot on Z′-axis and the corresponding values are 2Ω, 5Ω and 11Ω respectively showing the high conductivity of the N_GQD cells [45].

In conclusion, supercapacitive behavior in nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot has been investigated. Remarkable enhancement in storage capacity is observed. The concept of trap state which arises due to doping and edge states in the present doped graphene quantum dots is used to explain this enhancement in storage capacity. It is seen that the storage capacity is strongly related to the trap states generated in the doped graphene quantum dots due to doping and edge states. The conductivity data also shows that charge transport is also found to be trap state mediated.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M.M., S.B. and A.G. acknowledge DST-INSPIRE, and CSIR, New Delhi respectively for awarding their fellowships. SKS acknowledges DST, New Delhi, Govt. of India for financial support of project no. SR/NM/NS-1089/2011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.11.027.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
